title: NorthForge Onboarding Stitch-up & Runbook Huddle
preview: Kickoff for NorthForge Systems onboarding with Redwood CS/SE/AE. Main goals: validate networking/VPC plan, confirm security/compliance gating (SOC2, KMS, SSO), align on performance baseline for pilot, and agree next steps for runbook and benchmarks. Customer is leaning toward Dedicated for predictable throughput but wants cost tradeoffs and a quick hosted pilot. Action items assigned for runbook, perf tests, and security artifacts.
text: Kickoff for NorthForge Systems onboarding with Redwood CS/SE/AE. Main goals: validate networking/VPC plan, confirm security/compliance gating (SOC2, KMS, SSO), align on performance baseline for pilot, and agree next steps for runbook and benchmarks. Customer is leaning toward Dedicated for predictable throughput but wants cost tradeoffs and a quick hosted pilot. Action items assigned for runbook, perf tests, and security artifacts.

title: NorthForge Console: per-route usage + cost playbook
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-06-30
Start: 15:00 PDT
Duration: 48 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Price (Redwood AE), Samir Patel (Redwood SE), Ava Chen (Redwood CSM), Lina Torres (NorthForge CTO), Ravi Singh (NorthForge Infra Lead), Maya Ortiz (NorthForge PM)
---
Summary: quick console walkthrough focused on route-level dashboards, per-route cost breakdown, and a playbook for routing strategies (hosted vs dedicated vs private). Customer concerned about multi-tenant data residency, encryption at rest, and keeping per-feature unit economics low.
Topics: console navigation, route costing, batching and cache settings, private deployment considerations, next steps for pilot.
Action items: see metadata above.
---
Transcript:
[00:00:05] Jordan (AE): Hey everyone thanks for joining, uh we wanted to keep this to like 30-ish minutes plus Q&A, demo the console route usage view and then talk through a cost playbook. Samir's going to drive the demo.
[00:00:18] Lina: Thanks Jordan, we have 3 of us on from infra and product. We're mainly evaluating how Redwood helps us contain costs for conversational features in our app. We run a mixture of synchronous chat and background summarization.
[00:00:28] Ravi: Also important for us, some routes will touch PII, so VPC and KMS integration is a must, and we need to show it to compliance.
[00:00:35] Samir (SE): Absolutely. I'll jump into the console and show per-route dashboards. Quick note: apologies if the model names look a little off, our transcription sometimes shows Redwood Optimise as Opti-mize, et cetera.
[00:00:48] [00:00:48] Screen share starts - Console home
[00:01:05] Samir: Alright, top-left you can see 'Routes' - this is where teams map endpoint keys from your app to routes in Redwood. Each route gets its own telemetry, cost slice, and quality metrics.
[00:01:16] Maya: How granular is a route? Can we do per-feature, per-tenant?
[00:01:21] Samir: Yes. You can tag routes however you want — per-feature, per-customer, or combination tags. Then the console aggregates by tag for rollups.
[00:01:30] [00:01:30] Samir navigates to route list -> selects 'chat-stream' route
[00:01:34] Samir: Here's the 'chat-stream' route. Top panel shows RPS, p50/p95 latency, and token throughput. Below is a cost breakdown showing per-token cost, model mix, and an inferred cost-per-call metric.
[00:01:48] Lina: That inferred cost-per-call, is that including network egress and infra markup for dedicated?
[00:01:53] Samir: Good question. The default view is just inference unit economics: model cost + hosting markup if on Dedicated. We have a toggle to include infra overhead (GPU ops, egress) and also to apply your internal chargeback rates.
[00:02:06] Jordan: And we can overlay billing invoices so finance gets the same numbers.
[00:02:10] Ravi: When you say 'hosting markup', what are the knobs — can we see exact GPU hours used per route?
[00:02:16] Samir: Yes — we surface GPU-hours and percent utilization per pool and attribute it back to routes by sampling. It's not perfect down to the byte, but for cost allocation it tends to be within a small variance.
[00:02:28] [00:02:28] Samir clicks 'Cost drivers'
[00:02:31] Samir: Here's the 'cost drivers' pane — it's automatically inferring driver types: model size, token mix (long tails), and repeated completion calls that could be batched or cached. It also flags high-KV-cache miss rates — excuse me — KV cash miss rates in the transcript here, won't do that — KV cache miss rates.
[00:02:50] Maya: Can we see historical trend by rollout? Like if we do a canary, can we compare the canary route vs baseline?
[00:02:55] Samir: Yep, that's a first class view. If you use our rollouts feature you get canary vs baseline metrics and automatic regression alerts for both latency and cost per call.
[00:03:07] Jordan: Quick qualification: what's your expected qps for chat?
[00:03:11] Lina: Peak 60 qps, average 12 qps, but it spikes in events. We also have a background summarizer that runs many small batched requests.
[00:03:22] Samir: With those patterns we typically suggest mixed routing: hosted for low-latency public routes, Dedicated for steady high-throughput routes, and Private for PII-sensitive ones. You can pin model versions per route too.
[00:03:35] [00:03:35] Samir demonstrates 'Route tuning recommendations' panel
[00:03:37] Samir: This pane shows automated suggestions — batching window, cache TTL, and model quantization recommendations based on observed latencies and token distributions. For your summarizer we'd recommend increasing batch window from 20ms to 120ms to trade off a little latency for 3x token throughput.
[00:03:53] Ravi: Any quality regression risk when you quantize?
[00:03:56] Samir: We include an eval harness tied to your prompt sets so you can run A/B comparisons. The console will show Rouge/BLEU-like deltas and a human review queue for anything flagged.
[00:04:07] Lina: On compliance — does Redwood keep audit logs for admin actions and model rollouts? We need that for SOC2.
[00:04:13] Samir: Yes, we store audit logs, and Private mode can forward logs to your SIEM. We also do SSO/SAML, RBAC, and integrate with KMS for envelope encryption. For on-prem deployments we support HSM hooks but that requires an enterprise config.
[00:04:29] [00:04:29] Jordan: Totally — we'll include security artifacts in the follow-up.
[00:04:34] Maya: Pricing — we already use OpenAI for some features. How does Redwood pricing compare?
[00:04:40] Jordan: Redwood's hosted API is usage-based with per-token rates; Dedicated is a reserved capacity model with monthly commits, and Private is licensed. For a mixed workload we typically model blended cost — we'll send a route-level cost playbook that shows how you can reduce unit cost via batching and caching.
[00:04:56] [00:04:56] Samir pulls up a sample 'Playbook' document in console attachments
[00:04:59] Samir: Here's a sample playbook: step 1 identify heavy-token routes; step 2 set batching heuristics; step 3 apply prefix caching for repeated prompts and pin cheaper quantized models for non-sensitive traffic; step 4 measure quality with prompt-sets.
[00:05:16] Ravi: For PII routes we need assurances that model weights won't leak data, and that logs can be redacted — can you redact before sending to hosted?
[00:05:24] Samir: For hosted we offer optional field-level redaction and allow customers to opt into 'no retention' mode. For full control set up Private — then logs stay in your VPC.
[00:05:34] Lina: How hard is private deployment? We have an air-gapped environment for some customers.
[00:05:39] Samir: Private has an installer and an ops guide. Typical on-prem engagements are 6-8 weeks for regulated setups, shorter if you're using our VPC model in cloud. We'll scope this in the technical deep dive.
[00:05:51] [00:05:51] Jordan: Quick note — competitors often advertise lower token costs but then lack per-route visibility and rollout controls; that can cause hidden operational costs.
[00:06:02] Lina: Any customers with similar scale or compliance needs?
[00:06:06] Jordan: Yes — we have customers in fintech and health who run private control planes. I'll share a case study.
[00:06:12] Maya: If we wanted to pilot, what's the fastest path?
[00:06:16] Samir: We can spin up a trial org, ingest a weeks' worth of logs, run the playbook suggestions over a 2-week benchmarking window, and show projected savings. Typically that's 2-4 weeks to meaningful insights.
[00:06:30] [00:06:30] Samir opens 'Route cost breakdown export'
[00:06:34] Samir: You can export per-route CSVs, or connect our billing API to your internal FinOps tools. The CSV includes model mix, tokens, GPU hours attributed, and effective cost per call.
[00:06:45] Ravi: About attribution — you said you sample GPU hours and attribute back. Can you do exact attribution if we dedicate a pool per tenant?
[00:06:53] Samir: If you dedicate a pool per tenant attribution is exact. Sampling is only for shared pools. Dedicated + per-tenant pools is the cleanest for billing.
[00:07:03] Lina: We're also interested in routing rules — can we route by latency requirement and cost, like low-latency to smaller models, cost-heavy to quantized?
[00:07:12] Samir: Yes — smart routing policies support rules by latency SLO, cost threshold, and region. You can chain fallbacks: primary model -> cheaper quantized model -> short-circuit summary.
[00:07:24] [00:07:24] [Interruption as another meeting attendee joins briefly, faint audio]
[00:07:40] Maya: One more question — how does streaming billing work? We sometimes stream partial tokens for long responses.
[00:07:46] Samir: Billing is by token emitted. For streaming partial responses it's still token-based; the console shows per-stream durations to help with latency chargebacks.
[00:07:58] Jordan: Let's go over a hypothetical: your summarizer currently does 1,200 calls/min with average 900 tokens. If we batch and quantize you could cut token cost 40% and reduce GPU-hours by half.
[00:08:14] Lina: That would be huge. Do you have sample math in the playbook?
[00:08:18] Samir: Yes, there's sample spreadsheets. We'll populate them with your real usage after the trial.
[00:08:25] [00:08:25] Samir navigates to 'Alerts & regressions'
[00:08:27] Samir: Alerts can be set on cost-per-call, token burn rate, or model quality metrics. They can post to Slack, PagerDuty, or create Jira tickets.
[00:08:36] Ravi: Is there an API to adjust route config programmatically? We want to automate canaries.
[00:08:41] Samir: Yes — full REST API and SDKs. You can set rollout policies, change batching windows, or pin model versions in CI.
[00:08:49] Maya: For the eval harness, do you support human-in-the-loop scoring?
[00:08:53] Samir: We do — there's a human review queue and a way to tie labeled feedback back into model selection logic.
[00:08:59] [00:08:59] Jordan: Ok let's pivot — Lina, any blockers from procurement or security that would stop a pilot?
[00:09:06] Lina: Procurement needs a basic T&Cs review and a data processing addendum. Security wants SOC2 + encryption + KMS proof.
[00:09:14] Jordan: We'll attach our SOC2 report and the KMS integration doc. Samir will scope the Private checklist.
[00:09:20] [00:09:20] Quick live Q&A, a bit of crosstalk
[00:09:35] Ravi: One nuance — our live chat has sub-second SLOs. How often do you see Dedicated meeting sub-second for 75th percentile?
[00:09:44] Samir: For small models on dedicated pools we often hit sub-250ms p75 for simple turn-by-turn prompts. For longer contexts it's tougher. We can show latency SLO demos in the technical deep dive.
[00:09:58] [00:09:58] Samir shows 'Model profiling' panel
[00:10:02] Samir: Profiling shows tokens by layer — you can see where attention cost spikes and decide to trim context or use chunked summaries.
[00:10:11] Lina: Any known integrations with our stack — we use HashiCorp Vault and Okta.
[00:10:16] Samir: Okta SSO is supported and Vault for secrets/KMS is an allowed integration. For Private we can also do HSM plugs.
[00:10:24] Maya: How does rate-limiting interact with batching? If we throttle requests will batching increase latency drastically?
[00:10:30] Samir: Batching is adaptive — it respects per-API-key rate limits. If you throttle aggressively batching might wait longer, so we tune windows per-route. It's in the playbook.
[00:10:42] [00:10:42] Jordan: We're approaching 20 minutes — want to do a quick recap and next steps.
[00:10:48] Lina: Sure. From our side: we want pilot for summarizer + chat-stream, confirm compliance docs, and estimate cost delta vs OpenAI for same workload.
[00:10:58] Jordan: Great. We'll do: 1) send pricing + playbook, 2) Samir spin up trial org and load sample logs, 3) schedule SE deep dive for private deployment and latency SLOs.
[00:11:09] Maya: Can you include sample CSV export showing per-route GPU-hours?
[00:11:12] Samir: Yes — I'll attach an example export.
[00:11:16] Ravi: One more action: Lina will list routes that must stay private.
[00:11:20] Lina: Yep will do by end of week.
[00:11:25] Jordan: Perfect thanks everyone, we'll follow up via email with links.
[00:11:30] [End of meeting audio capture] 
---
Notes on transcription quirks: occasional speaker mislabels (some lines show 'Jordan' when actually Samir spoke), product names sometimes hyphenated (Redwood Opti-mize), technical terms like KV cache are occasionally transcribed as 'KV cash'. These are present in the transcript to preserve authentic Fireflies output.
---
Attachments referenced: console-playbook-sample.pdf (link in CRM), sample-route-export.csv (link), security/SOC2-redacted.pdf (link)
